name: WasmFX / Wasm Stack Switching
summary: WebAssembly proposal for typed continuations and structured non-local control flow that can compile fibers, lightweight threads, coroutines, generators, async/await, and effect handlers without privileging any one of them; reference implementation in Wasmtime, did not land in Wasm 3.0 but continues toward a subsequent milestone.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
WasmFX / typed continuations aims to extend WebAssembly with structured non-local control flow for efficient compilation of async/await, generators, continuations, and effect handlers. The proposed primitives — `cont.new` to create a fresh continuation from a function, `resume` to enter or re-enter it (passing values), `suspend` to yield from inside the continuation back to the caller — are structured to compile every higher-level concurrency primitive without privileging any one of them.

Distinct from C++/Rust async (which compile to one specific stackless state-machine shape per language) and from OCaml 5 effect handlers / Raku continuations (per-language runtime substrates): WasmFX is a Wasm-level analogue at the binary-format layer.

sections.title: Wasm 3.0 and the stack-switching milestone
sections.content:
**WebAssembly 3.0** (announced September 2025) consolidated several long-running proposals into one milestone: GC, memory64, exception handling with `exnref`, threads, SIMD, and JS string builtins all shipped cross-browser through 2024–2025. The stack-switching proposal, however, did *not* land in Wasm 3.0 — it remains a separate ongoing effort targeting a subsequent milestone, with reference implementation in Wasmtime (`wasm-tools` 2025+) and partial browser implementations behind flags.

The stack-switching primitives matter for the broader concurrency-substrate question because they let a single Wasm instance manage **multiple execution stacks concurrently** through typed continuation operations.

sections.title: Relationship to JSPI
sections.content: The interaction with **JSPI (JavaScript Promise Integration)** — already shipping in Chrome 126+ — is worth recording. JSPI lets Wasm code call JS Promise-returning functions and *suspend* the Wasm computation until the Promise resolves, without forcing the Wasm side to be authored in CPS or to drive its own scheduler. Stack switching generalises this: JSPI is essentially a single-purpose application of the same mechanism, specialised for the Wasm ↔ JS boundary. Once stack switching ships, JSPI becomes one consumer of it rather than the only one.

sections.title: Design implications for new languages targeting Wasm
sections.content:
Status (as of 2026-04): Wasm 3.0 is shipping in production; stack switching is in late-Phase Wasm-CG progression with WasmFX as the reference implementation.

The lesson generalises: **a cross-vendor binary format can ship structured stack-switching primitives** — historically a per-language concern — making language-runtime concurrency portable across Wasm hosts (browsers, Wasmtime, WAMR, Bun, Node). For new languages targeting Wasm, the design question is whether to compile to current Wasm 3.0 (CPS-transform required for resumable computation) or wait for stack switching to ship and use it natively.
